title: Summarize Journal Severity During an Incident
hook: Start with severity counts before opening every log line.
problem: An alert fired and you need to know whether the recent journal is mostly warnings, errors, or critical failures.
command: journalctl -p warning..alert --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager -o short-iso | awk '{count[$4]++} END {for (level in count) print count[level], level}' | sort -nr
starting_state: A systemd journal contains recent warning, error, and critical incident lines.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command counts journal lines by severity.
when_to_use: Use at the start of incident triage when you need a fast severity profile.
when_not_to_use: Do not use severity counts as root cause; follow up by grouping by unit and reading the timeline.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Counts followed by journal priority names.

commands: journalctl -p warning..alert --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager -o short-iso | awk '{count[$4]++} END {for (level in count) print count[level], level}' | sort -nr

shorts.title: Count journal severity first.
shorts.voiceover: Before reading every incident line, count warning, error, and critical entries. It gives you the shape of the problem in one pass.
shorts.caption: Incident triage starts with shape.

linkedin.question: When an alert fires, do you start with individual log lines or a severity summary?
linkedin.cta: Add this to your first-response checklist.

youtube.description: Use journalctl and awk to summarize recent journal severity without changing the system.

title: Group Journal Errors by Unit
hook: A noisy incident usually has a noisy source.
problem: Recent journal errors mention several processes and you need to see which unit or source is producing most of them.
command: journalctl -p err..alert --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager -o short-iso | awk '{split($3,a,"["); unit=a[1]; count[unit]++} END {for (u in count) print count[u], u}' | sort -nr
starting_state: The journal contains recent error and critical lines from multiple services.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command groups severe journal entries by source field.
when_to_use: Use after a severity summary to decide which service log deserves attention first.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume the noisiest unit caused the incident; it may only be reporting downstream failure.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Counts followed by unit or process names.

commands: journalctl -p err..alert --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager -o short-iso | awk '{split($3,a,"["); unit=a[1]; count[unit]++} END {for (u in count) print count[u], u}' | sort -nr

shorts.title: Find the noisiest unit.
shorts.voiceover: Group severe journal lines by source. It quickly tells you whether the incident is centered on the app, worker, kernel, or supervisor.
shorts.caption: Group errors by unit.

linkedin.question: What is your quickest way to find the noisiest service during an incident?

youtube.description: Group recent journal errors by source using journalctl, awk, and sort.

title: Print a Critical Journal Timeline
hook: Timeline beats guesswork when several failures happen close together.
problem: You need a compact timeline of severe journal lines with timestamp, source, priority, and message.
command: journalctl -p err..alert --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager -o short-iso | awk '{print $1, $3, $4, substr($0,index($0,$5))}'
starting_state: The journal contains recent error and critical entries around an incident window.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints a compact severe-event timeline.
when_to_use: Use when you need to compare app errors, worker exits, and supervisor messages in order.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it for full forensic retention; export logs through your normal incident process when needed.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Timestamped severe log lines with source, priority, and message.

commands: journalctl -p err..alert --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager -o short-iso | awk '{print $1, $3, $4, substr($0,index($0,$5))}'

shorts.title: Make a severe-event timeline.
shorts.voiceover: When errors cluster together, print only the severe journal timeline. It is easier to reason about order than volume.
shorts.caption: Order matters during incidents.

linkedin.question: During incidents, what do you use to reconstruct the first failing event?
linkedin.cta: Start with a compact journal timeline.

youtube.description: Create a compact severe-event timeline from journalctl output.

title: Show Context Around the First App Error
hook: The first error often explains more than the last one.
problem: An application log has many lines, and you need the few lines leading into the first error.
command: awk '{buf[NR%5]=$0} tolower($0) ~ /(error|exception|fatal)/ {for (i=NR-4;i<=NR;i++) if (i>0) print buf[i%5]; exit}' fixtures/incidents/app.log
starting_state: A synthetic application log contains normal lines followed by warning, error, and fatal events.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints buffered context around the first severe application line.
when_to_use: Use when the first failure context matters more than repeated downstream errors.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it when you need every occurrence; this intentionally exits after the first match.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Several lines ending at the first error, exception, or fatal log entry.

commands: awk '{buf[NR%5]=$0} tolower($0) ~ /(error|exception|fatal)/ {for (i=NR-4;i<=NR;i++) if (i>0) print buf[i%5]; exit}' fixtures/incidents/app.log

shorts.title: Find first-error context.
shorts.voiceover: Repeated errors can bury the useful clue. Buffer a few lines and stop at the first severe app message.
shorts.caption: First failure, with context.

linkedin.question: Do you inspect the first failure or the loudest repeated failure first?
linkedin.cta: Use this when repeated errors hide the original clue.

youtube.description: Use awk to show context around the first severe line in an application log.

experiments.variant_a: The first error is the clue.
experiments.variant_b: Stop after the first severe line.

title: Count App Errors by Minute
hook: A minute-by-minute count shows whether an incident is a spike or a drip.
problem: You need to see when severe application log lines clustered during an incident.
command: awk 'tolower($0) ~ /(error|fatal|timeout|exception)/ {minute=substr($1,1,16); count[minute]++} END {for (m in count) print count[m], m}' fixtures/incidents/app.log | sort -nr
starting_state: An application log contains ISO timestamps and severity-like message fields.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command counts severe application lines by minute.
when_to_use: Use when you need to compare an error spike with deploys, restarts, or external alerts.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a metrics replacement; it is a quick log-derived approximation.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.

commands: awk 'tolower($0) ~ /(error|fatal|timeout|exception)/ {minute=substr($1,1,16); count[minute]++} END {for (m in count) print count[m], m}' fixtures/incidents/app.log | sort -nr

shorts.title: Bucket errors by minute.
shorts.voiceover: Count severe app lines by minute. It tells you whether the incident was one sharp spike or a continuing failure.
shorts.caption: Logs can show error shape.

linkedin.question: When logs show errors, do you bucket them by time before reading each line?
linkedin.cta: Use minute buckets for quick incident shape.

youtube.description: Use awk to count severe application log lines by minute.

title: Count Request IDs in Error Lines
hook: Repeated request IDs can connect separate error lines to one failing path.
problem: An app log includes request IDs, and you need to see which failing request appears more than once.
command: grep -Ei 'error|timeout|fatal|exception' fixtures/incidents/app.log | awk '{for (i=1;i<=NF;i++) if ($i ~ /^request_id=/) print $i}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr
starting_state: A structured application log includes request_id fields on warning and error lines.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command extracts request IDs from severe lines and counts repeats.
when_to_use: Use when multiple log lines may belong to the same failed request or job.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it when logs lack stable IDs; group by source, path, user, or job name instead.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.

commands: grep -Ei 'error|timeout|fatal|exception' fixtures/incidents/app.log | awk '{for (i=1;i<=NF;i++) if ($i ~ /^request_id=/) print $i}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr

shorts.title: Group errors by request ID.
shorts.voiceover: If logs have request IDs, count them in severe lines. One repeated ID can connect the chain of failure.
shorts.caption: Correlation IDs are incident handles.

linkedin.question: Do your production logs include request IDs on every error path?
linkedin.cta: Use IDs to connect the failure chain.

youtube.description: Extract and count request IDs from severe application log lines.

title: Build a Deploy and Restart Timeline
hook: Deploys and restarts are incident landmarks.
problem: You need to compare deploy activity, restarts, and rollbacks against application errors.
starting_state: Incident fixture logs include application startup lines, deploy events, restarts, and rollback notes.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints matching event lines in timestamp order.
when_to_use: Use when an incident may correlate with a release switch, restart, or rollback.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as proof that deploy caused the incident; use it to focus the next investigation step.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Timestamped deploy, release, restart, and rollback lines.

shorts.title: Mark deploys and restarts.
shorts.voiceover: During incidents, deploys and restarts are landmarks. Pull them into one timeline before blaming the app.
shorts.caption: Landmarks make logs readable.

linkedin.question: Do you put deploy and restart events next to errors during incident review?
linkedin.cta: Build the timeline before deciding cause.

youtube.description: Use grep and sort to build a deploy and restart timeline from incident logs.

title: Spot OOM Kills in the Kernel Journal
hook: Exit code 137 often means the kernel has something to say.
problem: A worker exited during an incident and you need to check whether the kernel killed it for memory pressure.
command: journalctl -k --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager -o short-iso | grep -Ei 'out of memory|oom|killed process'
starting_state: The kernel journal contains synthetic OOM and killed-process messages.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command filters kernel journal lines for OOM evidence.
when_to_use: Use when services exit with code 137, restart suddenly, or disappear under memory pressure.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as a full memory diagnosis; follow up with memory, swap, cgroup, and process checks.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Kernel journal lines mentioning out-of-memory or killed processes.

commands: journalctl -k --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager -o short-iso | grep -Ei 'out of memory|oom|killed process'

shorts.title: Check for OOM kills.
shorts.voiceover: If a process exits with 137, check the kernel journal. The app log may only show the symptom.
shorts.caption: Kernel logs explain sudden exits.

linkedin.question: When a worker exits with 137, do you check app logs or kernel logs first?
linkedin.cta: Look for OOM evidence before guessing.

youtube.description: Use journalctl -k and grep to spot OOM kill evidence in kernel logs.

title: Find the Noisiest Incident Log Files
hook: The biggest log is not always right, but it is worth knowing.
problem: Several incident log files exist and you need to know which ones have the most lines before opening them.
starting_state: The fixture has multiple incident log files under fixtures/incidents.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command counts lines per log file and sorts the result.
when_to_use: Use when a service emits several logs and you need a quick noise map.
when_not_to_use: Do not confuse line volume with importance; a short kernel or deploy log can still explain the incident.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Line counts sorted from largest to smallest.

shorts.title: Map noisy log files.
shorts.voiceover: When there are several logs, count lines first. It tells you where the noise is before you start reading.
shorts.caption: Know the noisy files.

linkedin.question: Do you check log volume before opening multi-file incident logs?
linkedin.cta: Use line counts as a quick noise map.

youtube.description: Use wc and sort to find the noisiest incident log files.

title: Redact Secret-Looking Log Lines
hook: Incident notes should not copy secrets forward.
problem: Logs may contain token, password, secret, or bearer values and you need a safer view before sharing snippets.
command: grep -RInE '(password=|token=|secret=|Authorization: Bearer)' fixtures/incidents | awk '{gsub(/password=[^ ]+/, "password=REDACTED"); gsub(/token=[^ ]+/, "token=REDACTED"); gsub(/secret=[^ ]+/, "secret=REDACTED"); gsub(/Authorization: Bearer [A-Za-z0-9._-]+/, "Authorization: Bearer REDACTED"); print}'
starting_state: A synthetic application log includes a token-like field that should not be copied into incident notes.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command prints matching lines with secret-looking values redacted.
when_to_use: Use before pasting incident log snippets into tickets, chat, or reports.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat this as complete DLP; tune patterns for your real secret formats and still review output.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Matching log lines with sensitive-looking values replaced by REDACTED.

commands: grep -RInE '(password=|token=|secret=|Authorization: Bearer)' fixtures/incidents | awk '{gsub(/password=[^ ]+/, "password=REDACTED"); gsub(/token=[^ ]+/, "token=REDACTED"); gsub(/secret=[^ ]+/, "secret=REDACTED"); gsub(/Authorization: Bearer [A-Za-z0-9._-]+/, "Authorization: Bearer REDACTED"); print}'

shorts.title: Redact before sharing logs.
shorts.voiceover: Incident logs can contain tokens. Search for secret-looking fields and redact them before copying snippets into tickets or chat.
shorts.caption: Do not spread secrets in incident notes.

linkedin.question: What redaction check do you run before sharing incident log snippets?
linkedin.cta: Make redaction part of the triage habit.

youtube.description: Use grep and awk to print secret-looking incident log lines with values redacted.

experiments.variant_b: Incident notes should not spread secrets.
